codeSnapshot.label: pre_training.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader

# Causal Language Model pre-training (GPT-style)
# Next-token prediction: given [t1, t2, ..., t_{T-1}], predict [t2, t3, ..., t_T]

class CausalLMPretraining:
    """Minimal GPT pre-training loop."""
    def __init__(self, model, tokenizer, optimizer, scheduler):
        self.model = model
        self.tokenizer = tokenizer
        self.optimizer = optimizer
        self.scheduler = scheduler

    def train_step(self, batch_text):
        # Tokenise
        tokens = self.tokenizer(batch_text, return_tensors='pt',
                               padding=True, truncation=True, max_length=2048)
        input_ids = tokens['input_ids']   # (B, T)
        # Shift: inputs = t1..t_{T-1}, labels = t2..t_T
        inputs = input_ids[:, :-1]        # (B, T-1)
        labels = input_ids[:, 1:]         # (B, T-1)
        # Forward
        logits = self.model(inputs)        # (B, T-1, vocab_size)
        # Cross-entropy loss over all token positions
        loss = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()(
            logits.reshape(-1, logits.size(-1)),  # (B*(T-1), vocab)
            labels.reshape(-1)                     # (B*(T-1),)
        )
        # Backward
        loss.backward()
        torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(self.model.parameters(), 1.0)
        self.optimizer.step()
        self.scheduler.step()
        self.optimizer.zero_grad()
        return loss.item()

# Masked Language Model (BERT-style)
# Randomly mask 15% of tokens; predict masked tokens only
def create_mlm_batch(input_ids, mask_prob=0.15, vocab_size=30000):
    labels = input_ids.clone()
    # Mask 15% randomly
    prob = torch.full(input_ids.shape, mask_prob)
    mask = torch.bernoulli(prob).bool()
    # 80% [MASK], 10% random, 10% unchanged (BERT recipe)
    indices_replaced = mask & (torch.rand(input_ids.shape) < 0.8)
    input_ids[indices_replaced] = 103  # [MASK] token id
    indices_random = mask & (torch.rand(input_ids.shape) >= 0.8) & \
                     (torch.rand(input_ids.shape) < 0.9)
    input_ids[indices_random] = torch.randint(vocab_size, input_ids.shape)[indices_random]
    # Loss only on masked positions
    labels[~mask] = -100  # -100 = ignore in CE loss
    return input_ids, labels

eli3: Pre-training is the first phase of training a language model — before it learns to follow instructions or chat. The model reads billions of words from the internet, books, and code, learning one simple task: predict the next word. From this, it learns grammar, facts, reasoning patterns, and world knowledge.

It's like education before specialisation. A doctor spends years in medical school learning general biology, chemistry, and anatomy before specialising in surgery. A language model spends months pre-training on general text before being fine-tuned for specific tasks.

Two main approaches: causal language modelling (GPT-style: predict next token, left-to-right) and masked language modelling (BERT-style: predict randomly hidden tokens using context from both sides). GPT-style is now dominant because it naturally enables text generation.

whenWhy.title: Foundation model training (scale and compute)
whenWhy.body: LLaMA-7B pre-training: 1 trillion tokens, 6144 A100 GPUs, ~100,000 GPU-hours. GPT-4 (estimated): trillions of tokens, thousands of H100s. Pre-training costs $1M–$100M+. This one-time cost produces a model with general knowledge that can be fine-tuned cheaply for hundreds of downstream tasks. The pre-training compute determines the ceiling of model capability — Chinchilla scaling law: optimal compute allocation is roughly 20 tokens per parameter.

whenWhy.title: Causal LM vs Masked LM (CLM vs MLM)
whenWhy.body: Causal LM (GPT): predict token t+1 given t1,...,t. Autoregressive — naturally generates text. One loss term per token position. BERT MLM: predict 15% of tokens using full bidirectional context. Not autoregressive — requires two-pass for generation (slow and awkward). CLM has won: GPT-style models (LLaMA, Mistral, GPT-4, Claude) are universal; BERT is still used for classification where you don't need generation.

whenWhy.title: Data quality matters more than data quantity
whenWhy.body: LLaMA-1 was trained on 1.4T tokens of filtered web text and outperformed GPT-3 (175B params vs 65B params) on many benchmarks. Phi-1.5 (1.3B params) trained on textbook-quality data approached 7B model performance. FineWeb-Edu (HuggingFace): aggressive quality filtering of Common Crawl produces data that's 2–4× more efficient than raw web text. Data quality, deduplication, and domain balance matter enormously.

deepDiveIntro: The causal language modeling loss averages the negative log-probability of each token given all preceding tokens. Perplexity, the exponential of this average loss, is the standard summary metric — lower values mean the model is less surprised by the text on average. Pre-training minimizes this loss over a corpus of billions of tokens using gradient descent. [1]

The Chinchilla scaling law (Hoffmann et al., 2022) showed that for a fixed compute budget, the optimal model size and number of training tokens are approximately equal when expressed in appropriate units — roughly twenty training tokens per parameter. This overturned the prior practice of prioritizing large models: smaller models trained on more data are more compute-efficient and cheaper to run at inference. Llama models deliberately exceed the Chinchilla-optimal token count to produce a smaller, better-trained model. [2]

deepDive.heading: Causal Language Modelling
deepDive.body: The model receives a sequence of tokens and is trained to predict each next token given all preceding ones. During training all positions are processed in parallel using a causal attention mask that prevents any position from attending to future positions. At inference the model generates tokens one at a time, sampling from the predicted distribution over the vocabulary. A temperature parameter scales the logits before the softmax — lower temperature sharpens the distribution toward high-probability tokens, higher temperature increases diversity. [1]

deepDive.heading: Data Pipeline and Tokenisation
deepDive.body: A typical pre-training data pipeline collects raw text from web crawls, Wikipedia, books, code repositories, and forums, then deduplicates using approximate hash matching, filters for quality with perplexity-based or classifier-based rules, and tokenizes using byte-pair encoding with a vocabulary of 32,000 to 128,000 tokens. Documents are packed end-to-end to fill each context window without wasting space. Tokenizer choice affects how many tokens different languages and code constructs consume, with larger vocabularies generally being more efficient per character. [1]

deepDive.heading: Chinchilla Scaling Laws
deepDive.body: Hoffmann et al. (2022) fitted a parametric loss function to many training runs and found that for a fixed compute budget, training losses are minimized when model size and training token count are both proportional to the square root of compute. This translates to roughly twenty training tokens per parameter as a rule of thumb. Llama 7B was trained on over 140 tokens per parameter — deliberately exceeding the Chinchilla optimum to produce a smaller model that is cheaper at inference while still achieving high accuracy. [2]

youtubeLabel: How LLMs Are Pre-trained — Andrej Karpathy
takeaways: L = −(1/T)Σ log p_θ(wₜ|w₁,...,wₜ₋₁) — next-token cross-entropy over all positions. [1]
Chinchilla: optimal N=D ∝ C^0.5; train ~20 tokens per parameter for best compute-loss tradeoff. [2]
CLM (GPT-style) has won over MLM (BERT) for generality and generation capability. [1]
Data quality > quantity: filtering, deduplication, and domain balance matter enormously. [1]
Pre-training cost: $1M–$100M+; produces reusable foundation for cheap fine-tuning. [1]

edgeCases.q: Why do LLMs use byte-pair encoding (BPE) tokenisation and not character-level or word-level?
edgeCases.a: Word-level: fixed vocabulary, OOV (out-of-vocabulary) problem for rare words, morphological inflections. Character-level: no OOV, but sequences are very long (GPT-2 level: 4–5× longer), attention cost scales as O(T²). BPE (Sennrich et al., 2016): starts from characters, iteratively merges frequent pairs. Balances vocabulary coverage with sequence length. Common words become single tokens (economy=1 token); rare words decompose into subwords (unhappinesses = un + happi + ness + es). Vocabulary size 32K–100K achieves 2–4 characters per token on average. SentencePiece is a language-agnostic BPE implementation that handles multilingual text naturally.

edgeCases.q: What happens at the end of the token budget during pre-training?
edgeCases.a: Pre-training typically uses a fixed token budget (e.g., 1T tokens for LLaMA-7B). At the end: (1) the learning rate has been cosine-decayed to η_min ≈ 0.1·η_max; (2) the model has 'seen' all training data once or multiple times if repeating. For large models and quality data, repeating data (2–4 epochs) doesn't hurt much. For smaller models and large datasets, one epoch is standard. After pre-training, the model checkpoint is saved and fine-tuning begins. Continued pre-training (adding more domain-specific data after initial pre-training) is a common way to adapt foundation models for specialized domains (medicine, law, code).

sources.label: Brown et al. (2020) — Language Models are Few-Shot Learners (GPT-3) arXiv:2005.14165
Hoffmann et al. (2022) — Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models (Chinchilla) arXiv:2203.15556
